---
output:
  workflowr::wflow_html:
    toc: false
    self_contained: true
    css: adit/custom.css
site: workflowr::wflow_site
---

```{=html}
<style>
.pipeline-outer { margin-left: 0; padding-left: 0; width: 100%; display: flex; flex-direction: column; align-items: flex-start; }
.pipeline-wrap { font-family: -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, 'Segoe UI', sans-serif; margin: 1.5rem 0; width: 100%; }
.pipeline-title { font-size: 18px; font-weight: 600; color: #555; margin-bottom: 14px; text-transform: uppercase; letter-spacing: 0.05em; text-decoration: none; display: inline-block; }
.pipeline-title:hover { color: #111; text-decoration: underline; }
.pipeline-flow { display: flex; align-items: flex-start; flex-wrap: wrap; justify-content: flex-start; }
.pipe-start { display: flex; flex-direction: column; align-items: center; gap: 6px; padding-top: 14px; }
.pipe-start svg { width: 46px; height: 46px; opacity: 0.6; }
.pipe-start span { font-size: 14px; color: #888; }
.pipe-arrow { display: flex; align-items: center; padding-top: 35px; color: #ccc; }
.pipe-arrow svg { width: 40px; height: 24px; }
.pipe-node { display: flex; flex-direction: column; align-items: center; text-decoration: none; color: inherit; width: 161px; transition: transform 0.15s; }
.pipe-node:hover { transform: translateY(-2px); }
.pipe-node:hover .pipe-box { border-color: #666; }
.pipe-box { border: 1px solid #ddd; border-radius: 14px; overflow: hidden; background: #f9f9f9; width: 144px; height: 115px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center; }
.pipe-box img { width: 100%; height: 100%; object-fit: cover; }
.pipe-placeholder { display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center; width: 100%; height: 100%; }
.pipe-placeholder svg { width: 40px; height: 40px; opacity: 0.18; }
.pipe-label { margin-top: 8px; font-size: 14px; font-weight: 500; text-align: center; line-height: 1.3; max-width: 161px; color: #222; }
.pipe-sub-col { display: flex; flex-direction: column; align-items: center; }
.pipe-sub-line { width: 1px; height: 20px; background: #ddd; }
.pipe-sub-box { border: 1px dashed #ccc; border-radius: 10px; background: #fff; width: 130px; padding: 7px 6px; display: flex; flex-direction: column; align-items: center; text-decoration: none; }
.pipe-sub-box:hover { border-color: #888; }
.pipe-sub-tag { font-size: 11px; color: #aaa; background: #f0f0f0; border-radius: 3px; padding: 1px 6px; margin-bottom: 3px; }
.pipe-sub-label { font-size: 13px; font-weight: 500; color: #666; text-align: center; line-height: 1.3; }
.pipe-skip-arc { display: flex; align-items: flex-end; overflow: visible; justify-content: flex-start; }
</style>

<script>
var FOLDER = '<svg viewBox="0 0 40 40" fill="none"><path d="M4 12C4 10.3 5.3 9 7 9H16L19 13H33C34.7 13 36 14.3 36 16V30C36 31.7 34.7 33 33 33H7C5.3 33 4 31.7 4 30V12Z" fill="currentColor"/></svg>';
var ARROW  = '<svg viewBox="0 0 28 16" fill="none"><line x1="2" y1="8" x2="22" y2="8" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-linecap="round"/><polyline points="15,3 24,8 15,13" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round"/></svg>';
var PLACEHOLDER = '<svg viewBox="0 0 40 40" fill="none"><rect x="4" y="4" width="32" height="32" rx="6" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.5"/><circle cx="14" cy="15" r="3" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.2"/><polyline points="4,28 13,19 20,26 26,20 36,30" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.2" stroke-linejoin="round"/></svg>';

function renderPipeline(config) {
  var wrap = document.getElementById(config.id);
  if (!wrap) return;

  var titleEl = wrap.querySelector('.pipeline-title');
  if (titleEl) { titleEl.textContent = config.title; titleEl.href = config.titleHref || '#'; }

  var skips = [];
  config.steps.forEach(function(s, i) {
    if (s.skipTo !== undefined) skips.push({ from: i, to: s.skipTo });
  });

  var arcRow = wrap.querySelector('.pipe-skip-arc');
  if (arcRow && skips.length > 0) {
    var stepW = 161 + 40;
    var startOffset = 58;
    var svgW = startOffset + (config.steps.length * stepW) + 20;
    var svgH = 32;
    var paths = skips.map(function(sk) {
      var x1 = startOffset + sk.from * stepW + 80;
      var x2 = startOffset + sk.to  * stepW + 80;
      var r  = 10;
      return '<path d="M' + x1 + ' ' + svgH + ' L' + x1 + ' ' + r + ' Q' + x1 + ' 0 ' + (x1+r) + ' 0 L' + (x2-r) + ' 0 Q' + x2 + ' 0 ' + x2 + ' ' + r + ' L' + x2 + ' ' + svgH + '" stroke="#ccc" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="4 3" fill="none" marker-end="url(#skip-arrow-' + config.id + ')"/>';
    }).join('');
    arcRow.innerHTML = '<svg width="' + svgW + '" height="' + svgH + '" viewBox="0 0 ' + svgW + ' ' + svgH + '" overflow="visible"><defs><marker id="skip-arrow-' + config.id + '" viewBox="0 0 10 10" refX="8" refY="5" markerWidth="6" markerHeight="6" orient="auto-start-reverse"><path d="M2 1L8 5L2 9" fill="none" stroke="#ccc" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round"/></marker></defs>' + paths + '</svg>';
  }

  var flowEl = wrap.querySelector('.pipeline-flow');
  var html = '<div class="pipe-start">' + FOLDER + '<span>Data</span></div>';

  config.steps.forEach(function(s) {
    html += '<div class="pipe-arrow">' + ARROW + '</div>';

    var nodeInner = '<div class="pipe-box">' +
      (s.img ? '<img src="' + s.img + '" alt="' + s.label + '">' : '<div class="pipe-placeholder">' + PLACEHOLDER + '</div>') +
      '</div><span class="pipe-label">' + s.label + '</span>';

    if (s.sub) {
      html += '<div class="pipe-sub-col">';
      html += '<a class="pipe-node" href="' + (s.href || '#') + '">' + nodeInner + '</a>';
      html += '<div class="pipe-sub-line"></div>';
      html += '<a class="pipe-sub-box" href="' + (s.sub.href || '#') + '">';
      if (s.sub.tag) html += '<span class="pipe-sub-tag">' + s.sub.tag + '</span>';
      html += '<span class="pipe-sub-label">' + s.sub.label + '</span></a>';
      html += '</div>';
    } else {
      html += '<a class="pipe-node" href="' + (s.href || '#') + '">' + nodeInner + '</a>';
    }
  });

  flowEl.innerHTML = html;
}
</script>
```

## Cell Segmentation{.tabset .tabset-fade}  

Cell segmentation is the computational process of identifying and delineating individual cell boundaries within microscopy images, essentially teaching a computer to "**see**" where one cell ends and another begins. It is a foundational step in any imaging-based analysis pipeline, as all downstream measurements (transcript counts, protein expression, spatial relationships) are only as accurate as the segmentation that defines the cell units being measured. Poor segmentation directly propagates error: oversegmented cells artificially inflate cell counts and fragment expression profiles, while undersegmented cells conflate signals from neighbouring populations.

A persistent challenge is that generalised segmentation models, including those bundled in proprietary platforms like 10x Xenium Ranger, are typically trained on canonical cell morphologies: round, evenly-spaced cells in culture. In tissue, this assumption breaks down rapidly. Immune cells wedged between epithelial sheets, elongated fibroblasts, highly irregular tumour cells, and densely packed glomerular structures all violate the shapes these models expect. Indeed, 10x encourage resegmentation and have provided resources on how to approach this [link](https://www.10xgenomics.com/analysis-guides/segment-large-stained-cells-with-cellpose3) The result is systematic segmentation failure in precisely the cell populations that are often most biologically interesting. Custom model training on tissue-specific imaging data, as implemented in this module, is therefore not merely an optimisation - it is often a prerequisite for biologically meaningful results.

We've developed this **Cell Segmentation module** that can be optionally used in several imaging pipelines, including Xenium, COMET, IMC, mIF, etc. In this version of the module, we have opted for the cellpose method, as it provides a user-friendly UI and pre-trained starting models for training. 

```{=html}
<script>
var Segment = {
  id:        "pipeline-segment",
  title:     "Xenium spatial pipeline",
  titleHref: "xenium-index.html",
  steps: [
    { label: "Image Processing", 
      img: "assets/index-ims/xen-im-proc.png",
      href: "", 
      skipTo: 2.15},
    { label: "Cellpose training",   
      img: "assets/index-ims/cellpose-training.png", 
      href: "",
    },
    { label: "Bulk Segmentation",  
      img: "assets/index-ims/bulk-seg.png", 
      href: "" ,
      sub: { label: "Merge Masks", tag: "Optional", href: "" }

},
    { label: "Xenium Ranger",  
      img: "assets/index-ims/post-seg-col.jpg", 
      href: "" ,
      sub: { label: "Skip XenRanger", tag: "Quicker!", href: "" }
    }
      
  ]
};

</script>
```

```{=html}
<div class="pipeline-wrap" id="pipeline-segment">
  <div class="pipe-skip-arc"></div>
  <div class="pipeline-flow"></div>
</div>
<script>renderPipeline(Segment);</script>
```


<div>

### **Image Processing**

<span style = "font-size: 14pt">**Image Processing**</span>  

<img src="assets/index-ims/xen-im-proc.png" alt="xen-im-proc" style="width: 3in; float: left; margin-right: 0px;">

<div style="width: 6in; float: right;">

Image processing can be a fairly manual process if done in ImageJ or Qupath. It requires either a lot of point and clicking, or you require some programming skills to write macros. Moreover, it can be computationally slow/restrictive if the images are too large to open. As a result, we've developed a `python` function that performs the pre-processing required for cellpose. It currently processes images in ~1 minute, which outperforms manual processing in ImageJ (estimated to take about 15-20 minutes)

Cellpose allows for both a nuclei and a surface image. However, Xenium provides 4 images: DAPI, CD45/Ecadherin/ATP1A1, 18S, Vimentin/aSMA. Our function allows the user to select multiple *surface* stains to be combined into the single surface stain for segmentation, to provide as much data for a universal cell segmentation model. We also allow the user to select a lower resolution layer of the images, which can speed up the segmentation process. 10x benchmarks have indicated that reducing the resolution by one layer (from 0.2125µm per pixel to ...µm per pixel) have minimal impact on segmentation, but speeds the process up 10 fold. 

The output is then the segmentation-ready full image stack (full image segmentation - **Step 3**), and a series of random crops (model training - **Step 2**). 

</div>  
<div style="clear: both;"></div>
</div>  

<div>

### **Cellpose Training**

<span style = "font-size: 14pt">**Cellpose Training**</span>  

<img src="assets/index-ims/cellpose-training.png" alt="cellpose-training" style="width: 3in; float: left; margin-right: 0px;">

<div style="width: 6in; float: right; margin-right: 0px;">

This step is optional, but allows the analyst to generate a custom segmentation model through cellpose. Cellpose provides an interactive UI, where the cropped images are drag-and-dropped into the application window. A pre-trained model can be used to start the training, and several settings can be adjusted, such as cell diameter. A starting cell mask is generated and displayed, and the user can delete and/or add additional cell masks. This is then used to train a model and apply to the next crop. This is iteratively performed until the model is satisfactory. 

</div>
<div style="clear: both;"></div>
</div>

<div>

### **Bulk Segmentation**

<span style = "font-size: 14pt">**Bulk Segmentation**</span>  

<img src="assets/index-ims/bulk-seg.png" alt="bulk-segmentation" style="width: 3in; float: left; margin-right: 0px;">

<div style="width: 6in; float: right; margin-right: 0px;">

Whether using a pre-trained model or a customised model, this step points to the full-sized cellpose-compatible images and runs cellpose without the GUI to generate masks of the entire image, and iterate through each image. 

The output would be a 16-bit `.npy` file per image. These can then be used in **Step 4** to regenerate the Xenium outputs with the new cell segmentation. 

</div>
<div style="clear: both;"></div>
</div>

<div>

### *Merge Masks*

<span style = "font-size: 14pt">**Merge Masks**</span>  

<img src="assets/index-ims/mask-merging.png" alt="bulk-segmentation" style="width: 4in; float: left; margin-right: 0px;">

In some cases, hypotheses involving complex tissue environments requires the segmentation of multiple cell types of varying shape, size and density. This can be quite challenging for methods like cellpose to effectively segment every cell type. 

We therefore have the developed a method of merging two or more masks together. The challenge lies with choosing which mask to pull data from when pixels overlap, especially when you'll likely have the same cell segmented in multiple masks (and we also want to avoid duplication). We can therefore analyse the masks themselves to identify privileged cell masks to preserve. For example, we might want the static mask to be of larger structures like muscle or epithelium; on the transfer mask, we might only want to preserve immune cells. Therefore, we analyse the transfer mask using our data analysis module and identify the identities of immune cells. When merging the masks, we replace the pixels of the static mask with "immune cells" from the transfer mask. We can also optionally choose to include cells that do not overlap with any cells in the static mask.

[See this page for an example]()

</div>
<div style="clear: both;"></div>
</div><br>
